skill.name: uniprot-database
skill.description: Access UniProt through its REST API for protein searches, sequence retrieval, ID mapping, field selection, and batch workflows.
skill.summary: Search UniProt proteins, retrieve FASTA sequences, and map biological identifiers through REST API workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high and medium patterns, but review shows the external command and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from Markdown code fences, biological query syntax, and field names. The skill does include a Python script and examples that make intended requests to the official UniProt API, plus examples that write downloaded FASTA data to local files. No prompt injection, credential access, malicious endpoints, dynamic execution, or data exfiltration intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command and weak cryptographic algorithm alerts are not confirmed. The cited content is Markdown code fencing, UniProt examples, field names, and prior evaluation text rather than executable shell or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations contain documentation syntax and UniProt field descriptions, not calls to subprocess, eval, os.system, md5, or sha1. Targeted review found no executable command invocation or cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Intended Network Access to UniProt API
low_findings.description: The skill makes HTTP requests to the official UniProt REST API. This is expected for the skill, but users should understand that protein queries and identifiers are sent to an external public service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is consistently https://rest.uniprot.org or documented UniProt URLs. I found no suspicious domains, credential collection, or hidden exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: Example Downloads Write Local Sequence Files
low_findings.description: Reference examples write downloaded FASTA or stream data to local files. The examples use ordinary output files, but users should choose paths carefully when adapting them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and limited to storing downloaded protein data. There is no evidence of sensitive file access, path traversal, or persistence behavior.

low_findings.title: Polling Loop Has No Timeout
low_findings.description: The ID mapping helper polls UniProt job status until results or failed IDs appear. A remote job that never resolves could cause an indefinite wait.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The loop condition is clear and no maximum attempts or timeout is present. This is a reliability concern rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests Without Explicit Timeout
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python client calls requests.get and requests.post without timeout values. A stalled network connection could hang a calling workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The requests calls are directly visible and omit timeout arguments. The impact is operational availability, not confirmed compromise.

content.value_statement: Protein research often requires fast access to sequences, annotations, and cross-database identifiers. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through UniProt REST API workflows.
actual_capabilities: Search UniProtKB by protein name, gene, accession, organism, taxonomy, review status, and annotation fields.
Retrieve individual protein entries in JSON, FASTA, TSV, XML, TXT, RDF, Excel, and GFF formats where supported.
Map identifiers between UniProt and databases such as PDB, RefSeq, Ensembl, GeneID, GO, KEGG, and AlphaFoldDB.
Select return fields for smaller responses, including accessions, gene names, organisms, lengths, sequences, GO terms, and features.
Stream large query results and batch retrieve multiple accessions through the provided Python helper patterns.
Use reference guides for UniProt query syntax, return fields, ID mapping databases, and API examples.
limitations: Requires network access to UniProt REST endpoints and depends on UniProt service availability.
The helper script does not add request timeouts, retries, authentication, or persistent caching.
Large ID mapping jobs can take time, and UniProt mapping results expire after seven days.
The skill provides API guidance and helper code, not a graphical protein analysis environment.

use_cases.title: Retrieve reviewed protein sequences
use_cases.description: Find Swiss-Prot entries for target genes and collect FASTA sequences for downstream alignment or modeling.

use_cases.title: Map identifiers across databases
use_cases.description: Convert UniProt accessions to PDB, Ensembl, RefSeq, GO, or AlphaFoldDB identifiers for integrated analysis.

use_cases.title: Explain protein annotations
use_cases.description: Look up protein functions, domains, GO terms, organism data, and review status for teaching examples.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Protein
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search by gene or protein name.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search UniProt for reviewed human insulin entries. Return accessions, protein names, gene names, sequence length, and a short note on review status.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve FASTA Sequences
prompt_templates.prompt: Retrieve FASTA sequences for UniProt accessions P01308 and P04637. Explain which endpoint and format should be used.

prompt_templates.title: Map Protein IDs
prompt_templates.prompt: Map UniProt accessions P01308, P04637, and P69905 to PDB and Ensembl identifiers. Summarize any failed mappings.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Advanced Query
prompt_templates.scenario: Filter results by annotations and database links.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a UniProt query for reviewed human membrane proteins with kinase activity and PDB structures. Return recommended fields for a compact TSV export.

output_examples.input: Search for reviewed human hemoglobin proteins.
output: Returns reviewed human hemoglobin entries with UniProt accessions, gene symbols, protein names, and sequence lengths.
Highlights Swiss-Prot review status so users can separate curated entries from predicted records.

output_examples.input: Map P01308 from UniProt to PDB and Ensembl.
output: Submits an ID mapping job from UniProtKB accession format to the requested target databases.
Reports mapped identifiers, failed IDs, and the status workflow needed to retrieve results.

output_examples.input: Download reviewed human kinases as FASTA.
output: Builds a reviewed human kinase query and recommends the stream endpoint for complete FASTA retrieval.
Notes that large downloads should use rate limits, explicit output paths, and request error handling.

best_practices: Use reviewed:true when curated Swiss-Prot records are required.
Request only needed return fields to reduce bandwidth and parsing work.
Add timeouts, retries, and rate limiting when adapting the helper script for production workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not send private sample identifiers or unpublished data to public API endpoints without approval.
Do not stream large result sets repeatedly when cached local results would meet the need.
Do not treat TrEMBL predictions as manually reviewed evidence without checking review status.

faq.question: What does this skill connect to?
faq.answer: It connects to the public UniProt REST API, mainly under https://rest.uniprot.org.

faq.question: Can it retrieve FASTA sequences?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents FASTA retrieval for individual accessions, search results, and streamed result sets.

faq.question: Can it map identifiers to other databases?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers UniProt ID mapping to resources such as PDB, Ensembl, RefSeq, GO, KEGG, and AlphaFoldDB.

faq.question: Does it require an API key?
faq.answer: No API key is shown or required for the documented public UniProt REST workflows.

faq.question: What are Swiss-Prot and TrEMBL?
faq.answer: Swiss-Prot entries are manually reviewed. TrEMBL entries are unreviewed records that are often computationally annotated.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with confidential research data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Queries and identifiers are sent to public UniProt endpoints, so sensitive or unpublished data needs policy review first.
